summary:
The Assyrian conquest of the Kingdom of Israel (732–721 BC) was the two-stage Assyrian destruction of the northern Israelite kingdom, carried out first by Tiglath-Pileser III (who annexed the northern territories in 732 BC and deported their populations) and completed by [[event:1900|Sargon II]] (who besieged and captured the capital Samaria in 721 BC). The total deportation of the Israelite upper class — approximately 27,000 people according to Assyrian records — and their replacement with peoples from other parts of the empire was a deliberate policy of cultural disruption to prevent future revolt.

The deported Israelites disappeared from history, becoming the legendary 'Ten Lost Tribes of Israel' — a subject of speculation, religious yearning, and sometimes fraudulent 'identification' across Jewish, Christian, and Mormon traditions for millennia. The repopulated Samaria, with its mixed population practicing a syncretic religion, became the origin of the Samaritan people, whose tense relationship with the Jews who returned from Babylonian exile is reflected in the New Testament's story of the Good Samaritan. The Assyrian conquest of Israel had enormous theological consequences: prophets including Hosea and Amos interpreted it as divine punishment for covenant unfaithfulness, a theological interpretation that shaped Israelite and subsequently Jewish understanding of history as governed by divine judgment.
